1 min read
Just now

--

Over the last year, AI coding assistants have gone from a novelty to a core part of how many developers write software. Tools that once just autocompleted a line of code can now understand an entire codebase, suggest fixes across multiple files, and even execute tasks independently.
What’s actually changed
The biggest shift isn’t the AI itself — it’s how it’s integrated into the workflow. Instead of switching between a chat window and an editor, developers can now delegate entire tasks: “refactor this module,” “write tests for this function,” “debug why this API call is failing.” The assistant reads context, makes changes, and reports back.
Why this matters
For small teams and solo builders, this compresses timelines significantly. Work that used to take a day of manual debugging can often be resolved in minutes. For larger teams, it changes what a “junior” role even looks like — the entry-level bar keeps moving up because repetitive tasks are increasingly automated.
The catch
Speed doesn’t replace judgment. AI assistants are excellent at pattern-matching against code they’ve seen before, but architecture decisions, understanding business context, and knowing when not to over-engineer something are still deeply human skills. The teams getting the most value are the ones treating AI as a fast collaborator, not a replacement for thinking.
Testing this out
This is a test post while exploring Substack as a publishing tool — more posts (and a proper introduction) coming soon.
